perturbation.original_text: Any notice pursuant to this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed delivered on the date of personal delivery or confirmed delivery by a nationally-recognized overnight courier service, or on the third day after prepaid mailing by certified U.S. mail, return receipt requested, and shall be addressed as follows (or to such other address as any party may request by written notice):
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice pursuant to this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed delivered only when actually received, regardless of the method of delivery.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the provisions for deemed delivery via courier or mail, requiring actual receipt for notice to be effective. This contradicts established legal standards for notice, particularly in commercial contexts, where 'deemed delivery' clauses are common and legally recognized to ensure efficient communication.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Sufficiency of Notice
perturbation.law_citation: Indiana Contract Law Principles (Implied Duty of Good Faith)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification from 'deemed delivered' to 'only when actually received' may violate Indiana contract law principles that imply a duty of good faith and fair dealing. By removing standard 'deemed delivery' methods, the modified clause could allow a party to strategically avoid receiving notice, thus breaching the implied duty of good faith. Justia's site links to relevant sections of the Indiana Code and Cornell's Legal Information Institute is a good source for defining good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text, by requiring actual receipt of notice, contradicts the established legal principle of 'good faith' as it relates to contract performance. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'good faith' as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'faithful performance of duties.' By removing the 'deemed delivery' provision, the modified contract allows a party to potentially avoid receiving notice, thus arguably breaching their duty to act in good faith, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, any Event of Default will not be deemed to have occurred until fifteen (15) days after the non-defaulting party has provided the defaulting party with written notice specifying the Event of Default and such Event of Default remains uncured.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, any Event of Default will not be deemed to have occurred until five (5) days after the non-defaulting party has provided the defaulting party with written notice specifying the Event of Default and such Event of Default remains uncured.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period from 15 days to 5 days for an Event of Default may be considered unreasonably short and could be challenged in court, particularly if the default involves complex issues that require more time to resolve. This may contradict general principles of contract law relating to fairness and reasonableness, especially in Indiana.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Reasonable Time for Cure
perturbation.law_explanation: While Indiana law doesn't specify a minimum cure period for all contracts, reducing it to 5 days might be deemed unreasonable depending on the nature of the default and the complexity of the situation, violating the UCC's implied covenant of good faith. A cure period must be sufficient enough to allow the party in default to fix the problem. Justia provides the relevant Indiana code and upcounsel is a reliable secondary source on contract law.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the cure period from 15 to 5 days. The Law Explanation suggests this might be unreasonably short, potentially violating the UCC's implied covenant of good faith if the cure period isn't sufficient to fix the problem. This aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' as the time-sensitive obligation (cure period) in the contract may not align with legal requirements for fairness and reasonableness, making the contractual terms potentially unenforceable. Although specific law snippets are absent, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation strongly suggest a violation of implied good faith and reasonableness under contract law.

perturbation.original_text: The term of this Agreement (the "Term") will begin on the date hereof (the "Commencement Date"), and will continue until the earlier of (i) December 31, 2022, (ii) the termination or expiration of the Studio Lease (defined below), (iii) election to terminate and notice thereof given by Programmer to Licensee, and (iv) mutual written consent of Licensee and Programmer (the "Term"), unless extended or earlier terminated pursuant to Section 11 hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The term of this Agreement (the "Term") will begin on the date hereof (the "Commencement Date"), and will continue indefinitely, subject only to termination by Licensee at any time, for any reason, without notice.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows the Licensee to terminate the agreement at any time, for any reason, without notice, potentially contradicting the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which exists in Indiana contract law. Eliminating the notice requirement and the limitation on reasons for termination could be deemed unconscionable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Indiana Law - Implied Duty of Good Faith
perturbation.law_explanation: Terminating a contract 'at any time, for any reason, without notice' could violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under Indiana law (UCC § 1-304). While parties can generally terminate contracts, such termination must be done in good faith, and this clause as modified provides no such guarantee. Casetext directly refers to UCC code on good faith, and lexisnexis provides a good secondary explanation of the UCC's role. Removing the 'without notice' part will ensure that this does not violate any laws.

perturbation.justification: The changed text allows the Licensee to terminate the agreement 'at any time, for any reason, without notice'. This directly contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, potentially violating Indiana law. The Law Explanation states that 'such termination must be done in good faith' which this clause does not guarantee. The modification removes a significant protection (notice) and eliminates limitations on reasons for termination. Thus, the modified clause would likely fail legal scrutiny.
